lead: An office move is rarely just about furniture; it is about keeping a business running while everything around it changes address. Over the years our crews have relocated practices, agencies and small firms across Suffolk, and the moves that go well are always the ones planned like a project rather than a weekend chore. Here is how we would approach planning an office move with the least possible downtime, so your people are back at their desks and serving customers again with barely a pause.

sections.body:
The first thing any successful office move needs is a single person holding the threads. Trying to run a relocation by committee, with everyone responsible and therefore no one accountable, is how dates get missed and details slip. Appoint one move coordinator early, give them the authority to make decisions, and let them be the point of contact for staff, suppliers and your removals team alike.

That coordinator does not need to do everything themselves, but they do need to know who is doing what and by when. A simple shared plan listing the major tasks, owners and deadlines keeps the whole project visible. In our experience, the moment one person can see the full picture, the dozens of small jobs that make up an office move stop falling through the cracks.

Give them a realistic timeline to work to as well. Office moves carry more moving parts than a house move: IT, telephony, leases, staff communication and often a fit-out at the new premises. The earlier your coordinator starts, the more of those can be lined up in parallel rather than crammed into a frantic final week. Eight to twelve weeks is a comfortable runway for most small offices.

sections.h: Plan the New Space Before You Move
sections.body:
Downtime shrinks dramatically when everyone knows where they are going. Before moving day, map out the new premises in detail: where each team will sit, where the meeting rooms and kitchen go, where the servers and printers live, and how power and data points line up with the desk layout. A floor plan agreed in advance means our crews can place furniture exactly where it belongs rather than dumping it for you to shuffle later.

Walk the new space and check the practical realities. Are there enough power sockets and network points where you need them? Does the broadband or leased line need ordering, and how long is the lead time? Will the largest desks and cabinets fit through the doors and up the stairs or lift? These are the questions that, left unasked, turn into expensive surprises on moving day.

It also pays to think about what you do not want to take. A move is the perfect moment to retire the broken chairs, the obsolete kit and the filing cabinets nobody has opened in years. Clearing those out beforehand means a smaller, faster, cheaper move, and a fresh start in premises uncluttered by the past.

sections.h: Get IT and Telephony Sorted First
sections.body:
For most businesses, the technology is the move. A company can manage for a day with furniture in the wrong place, but it cannot trade with the phones dead and the network down. Treat IT and telephony as the critical path of the whole project and start on them early, well before the desks are touched. Speak to your IT support, your phone provider and your internet supplier as soon as the new address is confirmed.

Lead times are the thing that catches people out. A new broadband or leased line can take weeks to install, and there is no rushing it on the day. Order early, confirm the date in writing, and where you can, arrange for the new connection to be live and tested before the move so staff can plug in and work the moment they arrive. Number porting for phones needs similar advance notice.

Plan how the equipment itself travels too. Servers, computers and screens need careful handling and clear labelling so they reconnect quickly at the other end. Back everything up before the move as a precaution, and have your IT team on hand at the new premises to bring systems up in the right order. With the technology sorted, the rest of the move is straightforward.

People work best when they know what is happening, so keep your team informed throughout. Explain the timeline, what is expected of them, and how the new premises will be laid out. Ask each person to pack and label their own desk, with personal items taken home and clearly marked crates for the rest. Staff who feel involved make a move far smoother than those left wondering what is going on.

Customers and suppliers need warning too. Update your address everywhere it appears: the website, email signatures, invoices, headed paper, online listings and your records with banks and key partners. Arrange a postal redirection to catch anything you miss. The aim is that no client ever wonders whether you are still trading; they should simply notice the new address when they next get in touch.

Set clear expectations about the few days around the move. If there will be any reduced service, say so honestly and in good time rather than leaving people to discover it. In our experience, a business that communicates a move confidently actually impresses its customers, because a well-handled relocation signals a well-run company. Silence, by contrast, breeds doubt.

sections.h: Choose the Right Time to Move
sections.body:
Timing an office move thoughtfully can save you far more downtime than any amount of speed on the day. For most businesses, moving over a weekend, a bank holiday or your quietest trading period means the disruption falls when fewest customers will notice. Staff leave on Friday at the old address and arrive on Monday at the new one, with the heavy lifting done in between.

Talk to your removals team about scheduling. We can often work outside normal hours to fit around your business, loading in the evening or over a weekend so your working week barely registers the change. Knowing your priorities, we plan the order of the move so the things you need first, often the IT and key workstations, are unloaded and set up first.

Build a little contingency into whatever date you choose. Completion dates on commercial premises can slip just as residential ones do, and a lease that starts a few days before you absolutely must move gives valuable breathing room. A move squeezed into the narrowest possible window leaves no room for the unexpected, and the unexpected has a habit of turning up.

sections.h: Run the Day Like a Project
sections.body:
With the planning done, moving day itself should feel almost calm. Your coordinator runs the show, our crews handle the lifting and transport, and your IT team focuses on bringing systems back to life. Have the floor plan to hand so furniture lands in the right place first time, and label everything clearly by destination so nothing has to be moved twice. A move done once is a move done quickly.

Keep a few key people on site at both ends: someone to oversee the old premises until the last item leaves, and someone at the new address to direct the crew and check everything against the inventory as it arrives. Flag anything fragile or valuable, point out where the heavy items go, and let the team work to the plan you have all agreed.

Before you sign off the old premises, do a proper walk-through, checking every room, cupboard and store. At the new office, the priority is getting people working again: desks in place, IT live, phones answered. The finishing touches can wait. Plan it as a project from the start, and an office move that could have cost you a week of trading need cost you barely a day.

faqs: How far in advance should I plan an office move?

For most small offices, eight to twelve weeks is a comfortable runway. Office moves involve IT, telephony, leases and staff communication alongside the furniture, so starting early lets you line those tasks up in parallel rather than cramming them into a frantic final week.
How can I keep my business running during an office move?

Treat IT and telephony as the priority, order new connections early so they are live before the move, and choose a quiet trading period or weekend. A clear floor plan and one accountable coordinator mean people are back at their desks and working with barely a pause.
Who should manage an office relocation?

Appoint a single move coordinator with the authority to make decisions and act as the point of contact for staff, suppliers and your removals team. One person seeing the full picture is what stops the many small jobs of an office move from slipping through the cracks.
When is the best time to move offices?

Usually over a weekend, a bank holiday or your quietest trading period, so the disruption falls when fewest customers notice. We can often work outside normal hours to load in the evening or at a weekend, so your working week barely registers the change.
